case.name: Alex
case.history: The patient has a history of substance abuse and has been through rehab to overcome it. He has had issues with his family, particularly with his mother, where he has felt rejected and emotionally neglected. He has struggled with obesity since childhood, which has affected his self-esteem and body image. He also has a history of being victimized and bullied due to his weight.
case.intermediate_belief:
Helpless: I'm just not very good at handling stress and I have poor self-control, which is why I need to not put myself in stressful situations. 
 Unlovable: If I show my true self, people will reject me the way my mother rejected me.
case.intermediate_belief_depression:
Helpless: There's nothing I can do to change my situation. I cannot control myself. 
Unlovable: I don't deserve to be happy, so why even try to stay clean?
case.coping_strategies: The patient has adopted avoidance as a coping strategy by distancing himself from his family to reduce exposure to negativity and conflict. He has started scheduling pleasant activities and planning his day ahead of time in order to maintain a sense of control over his emotions and circumstances.
case.situation: Alex's cousin invited him to attend his upcoming wedding.
case.auto_thought: It will be stressful and negative; people will ask me questions I cannot answer or don't want to answer; my mum would be ashamed of me and critical of me as always; They don't want me there anyway, nobody likes me in this family.
case.behavior: Ignored the invitation and did not respond to the RSVP request. Ignored phone calls from family.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Welcome! I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not really sure what to talk about. I guess I've been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately, but I don't know why. It just feels heavy.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you're carrying some weight that feels hard to pinpoint. Can you share more about what "overwhelmed" looks like for you right now? What kinds of things are contributing to that feeling?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess it feels like a lot of responsibilities are piling up, but I can't quite figure out where to start. Sometimes I just feel like I want to hide away from everything. It's just this heaviness that makes it hard to focus on anything.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like those responsibilities are creating a sense of pressure for you, to the point where hiding away feels like a tempting option. When you talk about the heaviness and struggle to focus, what specific responsibilities or situations come to mind that are contributing to that feeling?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess I just feel like everything is coming at me all at once. There’s work stuff piling up, and then there are family obligations too. It feels like I’m trying to juggle too many things, and I can’t keep up. Sometimes I just want to escape from it all because it feels so heavy. I don't really know how to sort through it all.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It seems like you’re feeling really stretched thin with both work and family obligations, and that it’s creating this overwhelming sense of chaos for you. I can imagine how difficult it must be to navigate all those demands at once. When you think about wanting to escape, what do you think that represents for you? What would that escape look like?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about wanting to escape, it feels like I'm just searching for a break from all the pressure. Maybe it represents a desire to have some space to breathe and think without all these responsibilities weighing me down. An escape would probably just be a moment where I don’t have to worry about anything—like a quiet place where I can just forget everything for a little while. But I know I can't really run away from it all. It's just overwhelming to think about how to manage everything.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like the idea of escape is really about needing a reprieve and a chance to recharge without the constant pressure of responsibilities. That desire for a quiet space seems so valid, especially when everything feels so heavy. When you think about managing those responsibilities, what are some of the thoughts that come up for you about your ability to handle them?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about managing everything, I feel like I’m just not doing enough. There’s this nagging thought that I should be able to handle it all, but then I just get stuck. It’s frustrating because I want to be able to meet those responsibilities, but I feel like I’m constantly falling short. Sometimes I worry that I’m letting people down, and that just adds to the weight. It’s hard to see a way through it.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of pressure from both your own expectations and the fear of disappointing others. That feeling of not doing enough must be really tough to carry, especially when you're already feeling overwhelmed. Can you tell me more about where those thoughts of "not enough" come from? Have you felt that way in other situations before?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think those thoughts of "not enough" come from a mix of things. I’ve always had high expectations for myself, and I feel like I have to keep up with everyone else. It's like I’m comparing myself to others and wondering if I'm measuring up. There have been times in the past where I felt like I let people down, and I guess that still sticks with me. I just want to make everyone happy, but it feels like an impossible task sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like those high expectations and comparisons to others have really shaped how you see yourself and your ability to meet responsibilities. Wanting to make everyone happy is such a strong motivation, but it can also be incredibly taxing. When you think back to those times you felt like you let people down, how did that impact you then, and how does it feel to carry that memory with you now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think those moments when I felt like I let people down really stuck with me. It made me question my worth and whether I could ever meet my own expectations. Sometimes I replay those situations in my head, and it just adds to that feeling of being overwhelmed. I often feel like I’m carrying this weight of disappointment, both from others and from myself. It's hard to shake off that feeling, and it makes me anxious about facing responsibilities because I worry that I might fail again.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like those past experiences of disappointment have left a lasting mark on how you view yourself and your abilities. The weight of that anxiety about potentially failing again must feel really heavy, especially when you're already overwhelmed. When you think about that anxiety, what are some of the thoughts or feelings that come up for you in those moments? How do they influence how you respond to your responsibilities today?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about that anxiety, it feels like a tight knot in my stomach. It's this constant worry that I won't be able to handle everything, and that if I do fail, it would mean I’m letting everyone down again. Sometimes I feel this overwhelming sense of dread when I think about tackling my responsibilities. It’s like I want to avoid them altogether just to escape that anxiety, but deep down, I know I need to face them. It’s just so hard to find the motivation when all I can think about is that fear of failure.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like that knot of anxiety creates a really strong sense of dread around your responsibilities, making it difficult to find the motivation to tackle them. It’s understandable that you would want to avoid that discomfort. When you think about facing those responsibilities despite the fear, what do you feel is holding you back the most? Is it the fear of failure, or is there something else that feels significant?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think it’s really the fear of failure that holds me back the most. I just keep thinking about how disappointing it would be if I couldn’t meet my responsibilities again. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of worry, and it paralyzes me. I want to push through, but that fear makes it hard to even start. Sometimes I just feel so overwhelmed that I don’t know where to begin, and that makes me want to avoid everything even more.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like that fear of failure is really powerful for you, creating a loop of worry that keeps you feeling stuck. It makes sense that when you're overwhelmed, it becomes even harder to see a way to start. I'm curious, when you think about that initial step to tackle your responsibilities, what thoughts or feelings come up? Is there a specific task or situation that feels like it would be the hardest to approach?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think the hardest part for me is just getting started. Even thinking about it feels overwhelming. I often find myself staring at my to-do list and feeling paralyzed by all the tasks. I worry that I’ll mess up or that I won’t do them well enough, and that just adds to the anxiety. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle where I know I need to do something, but the fear of not measuring up keeps me from even taking that first step. I just feel so lost in how to break that cycle.

turns.therapist: Welcome! I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?
turns.client: I'm not really sure what to talk about. I guess I've been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately, but I don't know why. It just feels heavy.
